name: Uji-Shizuoka Japanese Green Tea Region

staple_foods.name: matcha / gyokuro green tea

primary_metabolites.protein_content: ~30 g/100g dry leaf [tea — shade-grown increases protein/chlorophyll; USDA FDC proxy]
primary_metabolites.ascorbic_acid: 180-260 mg/100g dry leaf [green tea — heat-sensitive, protected by shade; Deka & Bhatt 2013]

key_flavor_bioactives.caffeine_concentration: 2.5-3.5 g/100g dry leaf [matcha/gyokuro — Cabrera et al. 2006 J Am Coll Nutr 25:79S; shade-growing increases caffeine]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 20.0-28.0 g/100g dry leaf EGCG equivalent [shade-grown matcha — Weiss et al. 2003 J Nutr 133:3275S; highest catechin in the tea natural experiment]
key_flavor_bioactives.tannin_content: 9.0-13.0 g/100g dry leaf [matcha EGCG+EGC dominant — Weiss et al. 2003]

terpenes.linalool: 8-20 mg/kg dry leaf [Japanese green tea linalool — Kobayashi et al. 1994 Biosci Biotech Biochem 58:592; shade-grown retains more than unshaded]

organic_acids.malic_acid: 0.3-0.6 g/100g dry leaf [Japanese green tea — Hilal & Engelhardt 2007; moderate altitude, non-oxidized]
organic_acids.citric_acid: 0.1-0.3 g/100g dry leaf [green tea — Hilal & Engelhardt 2007]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 2.5-4.0 g/100g dry leaf as theanine [shade-grown matcha/gyokuro — highest theanine in pilot set; Cabrera et al. 2006; shading reduces theanine breakdown by blocking light-driven conversion to glutamate]

staple_foods.cultivar: Camellia sinensis var. sinensis (Yabukita cultivar, shade-grown for matcha)
staple_foods.notes: This RSU represents the third node of the tea natural experiment — the Japanese shade-growing (tana covering) technique introduces a THIRD experimental variable beyond altitude: light manipulation. Shade-grown matcha and gyokuro accumulate exceptionally high L-theanine (2.5-4.0 g/100g, highest in the natural experiment) because shading blocks UV-driven oxidation of theanine to glutamate. EGCG also peaks in shade-grown conditions (20-28 g/100g), higher than Darjeeling (18-22 g/100g) despite lower altitude (300m vs 2000m). This demonstrates that the tea catechin-altitude correlation is partially mediated through UV: shade artificially replicates some high-altitude shading effects. Uji (Kyoto) and Shizuoka are combined as a single RSU since both produce premium green tea on granite-derived acidic soils at similar altitude.
data_sources: Cabrera et al. (2006) — Beneficial effects of green tea, J Am Coll Nutr 25:79S
Weiss et al. (2003) — Catechins in shade-grown tea, J Nutr 133:3275S
Kobayashi et al. (1994) — Linalool in green tea, Biosci Biotech Biochem 58:592
Hilal & Engelhardt (2007) — Organic acids in tea, J Consum Prot Food Saf 2:414
Deka & Bhatt (2013) — Tea chemistry, J Food Sci Tech 50:874

staple_foods.name: short-grain white rice
staple_foods.notes: Japanese short-grain rice (japonica) has different amylose:amylopectin ratio than indica varieties — stickier, more cohesive, higher glycemic index. Koshihikari is the premium cultivar grown in this region. FDC 169760 used as proxy.

staple_foods.name: dashi kombu + bonito

primary_metabolites.protein_content: 5.1-8.3 g/100g [dried kombu — Fleurence 1999 Trends Food Sci Technol 10:25]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 1.0-2.5 g/100g [kombu Laminaria japonica — Ninomiya 1998 Food Rev Int 14:177; free L-glutamate is the defining flavor compound]
umami_compounds.inosinate: 0.4-0.7 g/100g IMP [katsuobushi dried bonito flakes — Ninomiya 1998; IMP+glutamate synergy produces extreme umami]

staple_foods.notes: Dashi (kombu + katsuobushi broth) is the foundational flavor system of Japanese cuisine and the best-documented natural source of synergistic umami. Kombu provides glutamate; katsuobushi provides IMP; their combination at a 1:4 ratio produces 7-8x the perceived umami of either alone (Yamaguchi & Ninomiya 2000). This is the most data-rich umami signal in the pilot set. Placed in a separate macronutrient category 'umami' (non-standard) — could also be considered 'fermented' as katsuobushi is mold-fermented. Compare to nuoc cham (RSU-19) and fish paste (RSU-12) for umami natural experiment.
data_sources: Ninomiya (1998) — Natural occurrence of glutamate in foods, Food Rev Int 14:177
Yamaguchi & Ninomiya (2000) — Umami and food palatability, J Nutr 130:921S
Fleurence (1999) — Seaweed proteins, Trends Food Sci Technol 10:25

staple_foods.name: miso

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 80-150 mg GAE/100g [miso — soybean isoflavones + fermentation phenolics; Yamabe et al. 2007 J Agric Food Chem 55:7749]

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.5-1.5 g/100g [miso fermentation — LAB contribution; Kobayashi et al. 2012 Food Chem 130:921]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.5-1.5 g/100g free glutamate [miso — Aspergillus oryzae protease activity during koji fermentation; Ninomiya 1998]

staple_foods.notes: Miso (fermented soybean paste) is produced by Aspergillus oryzae koji mold acting on soybeans + grain over months to years. The koji proteases generate free glutamate from soy protein. White (shiro) miso ferments 3-6 weeks; red (aka) miso 1-3 years — glutamate and Maillard compounds increase with age. This is the koji-fermentation umami counterpart to the dashi umami entry above.
data_sources: Yamabe et al. (2007) — Miso phenolics, J Agric Food Chem 55:7749
Kobayashi et al. (2012) — Organic acids in miso, Food Chem 130:921
Ninomiya (1998) — Glutamate in fermented foods, Food Rev Int 14:177

staple_foods.name: sesame oil

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 400-600 mg sesamol/100g [sesame oil — sesamol and sesaminol are key antioxidants; Suja et al. 2004 Food Chem 84:105]

staple_foods.notes: Toasted sesame oil is the defining fat of Japanese and broader East Asian cuisine — used as a finishing oil for flavor rather than a cooking medium. Sesamol and sesaminol (phenylpropanoid antioxidants) are heat-stable and accumulate during toasting.
data_sources: Suja et al. (2004) — Sesamol in sesame oil, Food Chem 84:105

known_processing_methods: koji mold fermentation (miso, sake, soy sauce)

notes: Uji (Kyoto) and Shizuoka green tea regions (~300m altitude). Granite parent material with highly acidic, fast-draining soils ideal for Camellia sinensis. Third node in tea natural experiment with RSU-20 (Darjeeling, 2000m) and RSU-21 (Assam, 100m). The shade-growing technique (tana nets 20-30 days before harvest) introduces a unique variable: light manipulation that biochemically mimics some altitude UV effects — L-theanine peaks here at 2.5-4.0 g/100g despite only 300m altitude. This RSU has the most data-rich umami dimension in the pilot set: dashi (glutamate+IMP synergy) and miso (koji glutamate) are both well-documented. High fermentation prevalence reflects deep koji-culture fermentation traditions.
